13: ho de telones makrothen hestōs ouk ethelen oude tous ophthalmous eis ton ouranon eparai, alla etypton to stethos autou legon ho theos hilastheti moi to hamarolio — the tax collector standing far off would not even lift his eyes to heaven but beat his breast saying God be merciful to me the sinner — hilastheti (be propitious/merciful to me, the aorist passive of hilaskomai) is the LXX atonement verb (used for the priest making atonement on Yom Kippur, Lev 16 LXX). The tax collector prays the Day of Atonement prayer in his own person: God, let atonement be made for me. The article (the sinner, not a sinner) suggests the man sees himself as the paradigm case of sinfulness.
1: elegen de parabolēn autois pros to dein pantote proseuchesthai autous kai mē enkakein — then Jesus told his disciples a parable to show them that they should always pray and not give up. Pros to dein (showing the necessity of — Luke’s dei construction used for divine necessity, applied here to persistent prayer) and mē enkakein (not to give up, not to grow weary and slack — enkakō means to behave badly from weariness, to collapse in the middle of an effort). The parable’s structure is an a fortiori argument: if even an unjust human judge eventually responds to persistent petition, how much more will God respond to the persistent prayer of his people?
2: kritēs tis ēn en tini polei ton theon mē phoboumenos kai anthrōpon mē entrepomenos — there was a judge who neither feared God nor cared what people thought. The double negative characterization (ton theon mē phoboumenos, not fearing God; anthrōpon mē entrepomenos, not respecting people) identifies the judge as outside both the vertical covenant structure (no accountability to God) and the horizontal honor-shame structure (no accountability to social pressure). He is the maximally unresponsive figure: if even he eventually responds, no possible petitioner could be harder to move than God.
3: chēra de ēn en tē polei ekeinē kai ērcheto pros auton legousa: ekdikēson me apo tou antidikos mou — there was a widow who kept coming to him with the plea: grant me justice against my opponent. Chēra (widow) is in Jewish law the paradigmatic vulnerable person who deserves special protection (Exod 22:22-23; Deut 27:19; Ps 68:5). Ekdikēson me (grant me justice, avenge me — the verb from dike, justice/right) — the widow is not asking for revenge but for the legal recognition of her legitimate claim. Antidikos (opponent in a legal case, legal adversary) — she has a specific legal dispute, not a personal grievance.
4: kai epi chronon ouk ēthelen; meta de tauta eipen en heautō — for some time he refused. But finally he said to himself. The judge’s refusal is extended (epi chronon, for a period of time) — the delay is part of the parable’s structure. Eipen en heautō (said to himself, an internal monologue) — the judge’s reasoning is made accessible to the reader; his eventual capitulation is purely pragmatic: not because of any ethical principle but because of personal inconvenience avoidance.
5: dia ge to parechein moi kopon tēn chēran tautēn ekdikēsō autēn, hina mē eis telos erchomenē hypōpiazē me — yet because this widow keeps bothering me, I will see that she gets justice so she won’t wear me out by her endless coming. Hypōpiazē (wear me down, exhaust me — literally “to strike under the eye, to give a black eye”; metaphorically to wear out by persistent battering) is a boxing term; the widow’s persistence is described as relentless physical battering. Eis telos (in the end, to the end, finally) — the phrase combines temporal and telic senses: the widow will keep coming until she prevails.
6: eipen de ho kyrios: akousate ti ho kritēs tēs adikias legei — and the Lord said: Listen to what the unjust judge says. Ho kritēs tēs adikias (the judge of unrighteousness — a Semitic genitive meaning the unrighteous judge) — the judge is defined by his moral character. Luke uses ho kyrios (the Lord) as the narrator’s title for Jesus (cf. 7:13; 10:1) signaling this as an authoritative interpretive comment on the parable. The instruction to “listen to” the unjust judge is ironic: the unrighteous man’s reasoning reveals something true about the nature of persistent petition.
7: ho de theos ou mē poiēsē tēn ekdikēsin tōn eklektōn autou tōn boōntōn autō hēmeras kai nyktos — and will not God bring about justice for his chosen ones, who cry out to him day and night? The a fortiori argument is made explicit: the unjust judge who cares nothing for the widow eventually responds; will not God — who loves his chosen ones (hoi eklektoi, the elect, those chosen in covenant relationship) — respond to their persistent crying? Hēmeras kai nyktos (day and night) — the continual nature of the prayer, corresponding to the widow’s continual coming. Kai makrothymei ep autois (and is he patient/long-suffering toward them?) — textual difficulty: does God delay or is he patient with his people while they wait?
8: legō hymin hoti poiēsei tēn ekdikēsin autōn en tachei — I tell you, he will see that they get justice, and quickly. En tachei (quickly, soon — either “without delay once he acts” or “soon in eschatological time”) — the assurance of swift divine action stands in tension with the apparent delay of the parable; the “quickly” is eschatological, not chronological. Plēn ho huios tou anthrōpou elthōn ara heurēsei tēn pistin epi tēs gēs (however, when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on the earth?) — the eschatological question with which the parable-application ends: persistent faith-prayer is what the Son of Man will look for at his coming; will it still be present?
9: eipen de kai pros tinas tous pepoithotas eph heautois hoti eisin dikaioi kai exouthenountas tous loipous tēn parabolēn tautēn — to some who were confident of their own righteousness and looked down on everyone else, Jesus told this parable. The audience identification is unusually specific: Luke names the attitude (pepoithotas eph heautois hoti eisin dikaioi — those who trust in themselves that they are righteous) and the behavior (exouthenountas tous loipous — treating others with contempt). The parable of the Pharisee and Tax Collector diagnoses the spiritual condition of self-righteous contempt, which is the opposite of the widow’s persistent, dependent prayer.
10: anthrōpoi dyo anebēsan eis to hieron proseuxasthai, ho heis pharisaios kai ho heteros telōnēs — two men went up to the temple to pray, one a Pharisee and the other a tax collector. The two figures represent opposite ends of Jewish religious respectability: the Pharisee is the model of Torah-observance and religious discipline; the tax collector is a collaborator with Rome and a ritual outcast. Both go to the same temple, both to pray — the parable is about two kinds of prayer, not two moral categories of human being.
11: ho pharisaios statheis pros heauton tauta proseucheto: ho theos, eucharistō soi hoti ouk eimi hōsper hoi loipoi tōn anthrōpōn — the Pharisee stood and prayed about himself: God, I thank you that I am not like other people. Pros heauton (about/to himself) — a textual and interpretive crux: does he pray “about himself” (the prayer’s content is self-referential) or “to himself” (the prayer never leaves the self, never reaches God)? Both senses may be intended. The prayer is formally a thanksgiving (eucharistō) but its content is comparative self-praise: his virtue is defined by contrast with others. The negative list (not like robbers, evildoers, adulterers, or this tax collector) is his spiritual identity.
12: nēsteuō dis tou sabbatou, apodekatō panta hosa ktōmai — I fast twice a week and give a tenth of all I get. The Pharisee’s positive credentials: fasting twice weekly (dis tou sabbatou) exceeded the Torah requirement (Yom Kippur) and the common practice (Mondays and Thursdays were voluntary fast days for the pious); tithing all possessions (panta hosa ktōmai, all that I acquire) extended the Torah tithe to include untithed produce bought at market. Both are genuine acts of religious discipline — the problem is not the practices themselves but their function as the basis of self-comparison and divine claim.
14: legō hymin, katebē houtos dedikaiōmenos eis ton oikon autou par ekeinon — I tell you that this man, rather than the other, went home justified before God. Dedikaiōmenos (having been justified, declared righteous — the perfect passive participle of dikaioō) — the same term that Paul uses for forensic justification in Romans and Galatians. The tax collector who prayed the atonement prayer (v.13 — “God, be propitious to me the sinner”) is declared righteous; the Pharisee who catalogued his virtues is not. The parable enacts the reversal-principle that closes it: pas ho hypsōn heauton tapeinōthēsetai, ho de tapeinōn heauton hypsōthēsetai (everyone who exalts himself will be humbled; the one who humbles himself will be exalted).
15: prosepheron de autō kai ta brephē hina autōn haptētai; idontes de hoi mathētai epetimōn autois — people were also bringing babies to Jesus for him to place his hands on them. When the disciples saw this, they rebuked them. Brephē (infants, newborns — the earliest stage of life) distinguishes Luke’s version from Matthew’s and Mark’s paidia (children); the adults bringing infants to Jesus is a more extreme case of the kingdom’s reception. The disciples’ rebuke (epetimōn) reflects their role-protective instinct: the teacher’s time and attention should not be consumed by infants. Jesus’s reversal will make their rebuke the occasion for a kingdom lesson.
16: ho de Iēsous proskalesamenos auta eipen: aphete ta paidia erchesthai pros me kai mē kōluete auta — but Jesus called the children to him and said: Let the little children come to me and do not hinder them. Aphete (let, release — the same root as aphesis, forgiveness/release) and mē kōluete (do not hinder) — Jesus removes both the disciples’ barrier and the prohibition. Tōn gar toioutōn estin hē basileia tou theou (for the kingdom of God belongs to such as these) — the genitive tōn toioutōn (of such as these) is the ground of the welcome: the kingdom is constituted by those who have this quality of receptive dependence.
17: amēn legō hymin, hos an mē dexētai tēn basileian tou theou hōs paidion, ou mē eiselthē eis autēn — truly I tell you, anyone who will not receive the kingdom of God like a little child will never enter it. Hōs paidion (like a child, as a child) — the child is not a model of innocence or purity but of receptivity: a child receives a gift without any contribution of merit or any transaction of exchange. The kingdom is not earned, negotiated, or deserved — it is received. Ou mē eiselthē (will certainly not enter — the double negative ou mē is the strongest negative in Greek) — the double-strength negative emphasizes that no alternative path of entry exists.
18: kai epērōtēsen tis auton archōn legōn: didaskale agathe, ti poiēsas zōēn aiōnion klēronomiō — a certain ruler asked him: Good teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life? Archōn (ruler — Luke specifies he is a ruler, giving him social authority; Matthew and Mark say “someone” or “a young man”). Didaskale agathe (good teacher) — the address to which Jesus responds with a counterquestion about goodness. Ti poiēsas (what having done, what must I do?) — the question assumes the active merit-earning model of eternal life; the ruler is looking for an achievable task. Jesus will expose the assumption.
19: eipen de autō ho Iēsous: ti me legeis agathon? oudeis agathos ei mē heis ho theos — why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone. The counterquestion is not a denial of Jesus’s own goodness but a redirection: if “good” is a meaningful category in the absolute sense, it belongs to God alone. The ruler’s complimentary address has used “good” as a social honorific; Jesus insists on its theological weight. The implicit question is: if God alone is good, and if the ruler recognizes Jesus as good, what does that say about who Jesus is? The Christological implication is not explicit but hangs in the air.
20: tas entolas oidas: mē moicheusēs, mē phoneusēs, mē klepsēs, mē pseudomarturēsēs, tima ton patera sou kai tēn mētera — you know the commandments: do not commit adultery, do not murder, do not steal, do not give false testimony, honor your father and mother. The subset of commandments Jesus cites is the second table of the Decalogue — the social/horizontal commandments (duties toward other humans). The first table (duties toward God) is conspicuously absent; Jesus will expose the ruler’s failure precisely in the area of the first commandment: his god is his wealth.
21: ho de eipen: tauta panta ephylaxamēn ek neotētos — all these I have kept since I was a boy. The ruler’s claim of lifelong commandment-keeping is not challenged by Jesus; the statement is accepted as his self-assessment. The MKT “since I was a boy” (ek neotētos, from youth) may indicate teenage years when a Jewish boy came under full Torah obligation. The claim is not necessarily false — a man of wealth and social position could maintain the observable social commandments. What he lacks is beyond observable compliance.
22: akousas de ho Iēsous eipen autō: eti hen soi leipei; panta hosa echeis pōlēson kai diaskorpison ptōchois — when Jesus heard this, he said to him: You still lack one thing. Sell everything you have and give to the poor. Hen leipei (one thing is lacking) — the singular lack contrasts with the plural commandments kept; the one missing thing is more significant than all the kept ones combined. Pōlēson kai diaskorpison ptōchois (sell and distribute to the poor) — the command is total: panta hosa echeis (everything you have). Kai deuro akolouthei moi (and come, follow me) — the follow-me invitation is the completion; the selling is the prerequisite for discipleship, not the merit-act that earns eternal life.
23: ho de akousas tauta perilypos egeneto, ēn gar plousios sphodra — when he heard this, he became very sad, because he was very wealthy. Perilypos (very sad, deeply grieved — peri- intensifying lypos/grief; the same word used for Jesus in Gethsemane, Mark 14:34) — the ruler’s grief is genuine; he genuinely desired eternal life and genuinely cannot bring himself to relinquish the wealth to which he is deeply attached. Ēn plousios sphodra (he was very wealthy) — the “very” intensifies the attachment; his wealth is not marginal but central to his identity and social power.
24: idōn de auton ho Iēsous perilypon genomenon eipen: pōs dyskōlōs hoi ta chrēmata echontes eis tēn basileian tou theou eisporeuontai — Jesus looked at him and said: How hard it is for the rich to enter the kingdom of God! Dyskōlōs (with difficulty, hard — from dys- bad + koilon the stomach; one who is hard to please even with food, hence finicky, difficult). The observation is compassionate and diagnostic: Jesus looks at the departing ruler and reads in him the universal problem of wealth as attachment. The difficulty is not moral condemnation but structural: wealth creates an alternative trust-system that competes with trust in God.
25: eukolōteron gar estin kamēlon dia trēmatos belonēs eiselthein ē plousion eis tēn basileian tou theou eiselthein — indeed, it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for someone who is rich to enter the kingdom of God. The hyperbole of the camel through a needle’s eye (kamēlos... trēmatos belonēs) is the largest animal in Palestine passing through the smallest aperture — the most vivid impossibility available in the cultural context. The attempts to domesticate this (a “camel” rope, a “Needle’s Gate” at Jerusalem) lack historical support; the image is deliberately, humorously extreme to make the point about the difficulty of wealth-detachment.
26: eipan de hoi akousantes: kai tis dynatai sōthēnai — those who heard this asked: Who then can be saved? The crowd’s conclusion is logical: if the wealthy are paradigmatically unable to enter the kingdom (and wealth was widely taken as a sign of divine blessing), then no one can qualify. The question exposes the assumption that the wealthy are the most likely to be saved; Jesus’s response will dismantle both the wealth-blessing assumption and the human-effort model of salvation.
27: ho de eipen: ta adynata para anthrōpois dynata para tō theō estin — Jesus replied: What is impossible with man is possible with God. Adynata para anthrōpois (impossible alongside/with humans, humanly impossible) and dynata para tō theō (possible alongside/with God, divinely possible) — the framing shifts the locus of possibility from human effort to divine action. The salvation of the wealthy is not impossible because of wealth itself but because human attachment to wealth makes self-rescue impossible; what God can do is not limited by what wealth-attachment has blocked.
28: eipen de ho Petros: idou hēmeis aphentes ta idia ēkolouthēsamen soi — Peter said to him: We have left all we had to follow you. Peter’s statement is factually accurate (they left their fishing nets, tax booth, family) and contextually pointed: the disciples have done what the ruler could not do. Aphentes ta idia (having left our own things) — the aorist participle emphasizes the completed act of leaving; Peter is identifying the disciples as those who have already crossed the threshold the ruler failed to cross.
29: ho de eipen autois: amēn legō hymin hoti oudeis estin hos aphēken oikian ē gynaika ē adelphous ē goneis ē tekna — truly I tell you, no one who has left home or wife or brothers or parents or children. Luke’s list includes gynaika (wife) which the parallel in Mark 10:29 lacks in some manuscripts; the Lukan expansion of the list to include wife reflects the more radical demands of Luke’s travel-narrative discipleship. The “for the sake of the kingdom of God” (heneken tēs basileias tou theou) replaces Mark’s “for me and the gospel.”
30: hos ouchi mē apolampsē pollaplasiona en tō kairō toutō kai en tō aiōni tō erchomenō zōēn aiōnion — will fail to receive many times as much in this age, and in the age to come eternal life. Pollaplasiona (many times over, manifold) — the promise is extravagant: the leaving of family and property for the kingdom is met with a manifold return already in “this age” (en tō kairō toutō — the present eschatological age inaugurated by Jesus). En tō aiōni tō erchomenō zōēn aiōnion (in the coming age eternal life) — the double promise (manifold now, eternal life then) encompasses both the present and the future dimensions of kingdom participation.
31: paralab̄ōn de tous dōdeka eipen pros autous: idou anabainomen eis Ierousalēm kai telesthēsetai panta ta gegrammena dia tōn prophētōn tō huiō tou anthrōpou — Jesus took the Twelve aside and told them: We are going up to Jerusalem, and everything written by the prophets about the Son of Man will be fulfilled. This is the third formal passion prediction in Luke (cf. 9:22; 9:43-45) and the most detailed. Telesthēsetai (will be completed, fulfilled — from teleō, to complete, bring to the appointed end) — the passion is a teleological completion: the Son of Man’s prophetically scripted destiny is being accomplished. Ta gegrammena dia tōn prophētōn (what has been written through the prophets) makes the passion a scriptural necessity.
32: paradothēsetai gar tois ethnesin kai empaichthēsetai kai hybristhēsetai kai emptysthēsetai — he will be handed over to the Gentiles. They will mock him, insult him and spit on him. Paradothēsetai tois ethnesin (will be handed over to the Gentiles) — the divine passive of the handing-over (paradidōmi) is the passion’s central verb; the handing-over to Rome is the mechanism of the scriptural fulfillment. The four acts of humiliation (mock, insult, spit, flog — a four-verb passion inventory) correspond to the Servant songs and Psalm 22: the Son of Man’s passion is the Servant’s suffering enacted.
33: kai mastigōsantes apoktennousin auton kai tē hēmera tē tritē anastēsetai — they will flog him and kill him. On the third day he will rise again. Mastigōsantes (having flogged) anticipates Pilate’s chastisement before the crowd (23:16,22). Apoktennousin (they will kill) — the divine passive is abandoned here for the active voice, placing human agency explicitly in the passion’s killing. Tē hēmera tē tritē anastēsetai (on the third day he will rise again) — the resurrection promise is embedded in the passion prediction; the announcement is not merely of death but of death-and-resurrection as a single event with two phases.
34: kai autoi ouden toutōn synēkan — the disciples did not understand any of this. The triple incomprehension formula (ouden toutōn synēkan, they understood nothing of these things; kekrymmenon ap autōn to rhēma touto, this word was hidden from them; ouk eginōskon ta legomena, they did not know what was being said) is uniquely intensive in Luke. The hiddenness is providential (kekrymmenon, passive — hidden by divine action): they cannot understand the passion-resurrection until the resurrection itself opens their understanding (24:45). The three-fold incomprehension formula protects the resurrection as the hermeneutical key to the passion.
35: egeneto de en tō engizein auton eis Iericho typhlos tis ekathēto para tēn hodon epaitōn — as Jesus approached Jericho, a blind man was sitting by the roadside begging. The blind beggar episode immediately follows the third passion prediction and the disciples’ incomprehension, creating a contrast: the disciples cannot see what Jesus has just told them; the blind man will receive sight by faith. Para tēn hodon epaitōn (by the road begging) — the roadside is the natural location for beggars who depend on the generosity of passing travelers; the Jericho road, the route from the Jordan valley to Jerusalem, was well-traveled.
36: akousas de ochlou diaporeuomenou epynthaneto ti eiē touto — when he heard the crowd going by, he asked what was happening. Epynthaneto (he was asking, inquiring — imperfect, continuous asking) — the blind man depends entirely on auditory information; hearing the crowd’s movement, he asks for a visual report. Ti eiē touto (what this might be — optative of indirect question) — the polite form of an eager inquiry. His dependence on others for information about the visible world is the physical expression of his general condition; his healing will be both literal and symbolic.
37: apēngeilan de autō hoti Iēsous ho Nazōraios parerchestai — they told him: Jesus of Nazareth is passing by. Iēsous ho Nazōraios (Jesus the Nazarene — the geographic identification that became a title; Acts 2:22; 3:6; 4:10 all use this form as a proper identification of the risen Jesus in early preaching). Parerchestai (is passing by) — the present tense conveys urgency: the moment of passage is happening now. The blind man’s response will be immediate and desperate.
38: kai eboēsen legōn: Iēsou huie David, eleēson me — he called out: Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me! Huie David (Son of David) is the messianic title from the Davidic covenant promise (2 Sam 7:14; Ps 89:3-4; Isa 11:1-3); in the Synoptic tradition, it is the title used by those who recognize Jesus’s Messianic identity from outside the inner circle (also Matt 9:27; 15:22; 20:30). The blind man’s cry is a Christological confession that the disciples have struggled to articulate; his blindness has not obscured his recognition of who Jesus is.
39: kai hoi proagontes epetimōn autō hina sigēsē; autos de pollō mallon ekrazen: huie David, eleēson me — those who led the way rebuked him and told him to be quiet, but he shouted all the more: Son of David, have mercy on me! The crowd’s rebuke (epetimōn) creates a social pressure to silence — the same verb used for the disciples’ rebuke of those bringing children (v.15). The blind man’s response (pollō mallon ekrazen — he shouted all the more) is the opposite of compliance: the resistance increases the volume of his petition. His persistence parallels the widow’s (18:3) and is the parable’s enacted embodiment.
40: statheis de ho Iēsous ekeleusen auton achthēnai pros auton — Jesus stopped and ordered the man to be brought to him. Statheis (having stopped, standing still) — the entire procession toward Jerusalem pauses; Jesus halts the journey for the blind beggar. The stopping is a narrative gesture of compassion: the Son of David stops for the one who called on him by that title. Ekeleusen achthēnai (commanded him to be brought) — the summons reverses the crowd’s exclusion; the one they tried to silence is now summoned to the center.
41: eggisantos de autou epērōtēsen auton: ti soi theleis poiēsō? ho de eipen: kyrie, hina anablepō — what do you want me to do for you? Lord, I want to see. The open question (ti soi theleis poiēsō, what do you want me to do for you?) — also asked by Jesus to James and John (Mark 10:36), but there the request is for thrones. Here the blind man’s desire is simply and completely expressed: hina anablepō (that I might see again — the ana- prefix suggests restored sight, implying he was not born blind). His request is clear, self-aware, and entirely dependent: he wants what only Jesus can give.
42: kai ho Iēsous eipen autō: anablepson; hē pistis sou sesōken se — Jesus said to him: Receive your sight; your faith has saved you. Anablepson (look up, see again — the same root as the petition) — the command is the fulfillment of the request, word-for-word. Hē pistis sou sesōken se (your faith has saved you) — the formula of salvation (also 7:50; 8:48; 17:19) confirms this as more than physical healing: the blind man’s pistis (faith, trust — expressed in calling on the Son of David and persisting despite opposition) is the receptive medium through which saving power flows.
43: kai parachrēma aneblepsen kai ēkolouthei autō doxazōn ton theon — immediately he received his sight and followed Jesus, praising God. Parachrēma (immediately) — Luke’s favorite term for miraculous instantaneity (also 1:64; 4:39; 5:25; 8:44; 13:13; 19:11). Ēkolouthei autō (he was following him, imperfect — continuous following) — the healed man does not go home; he joins the procession to Jerusalem. His following is the embodiment of discipleship: the one who was sitting by the road is now walking the road with Jesus. Doxazōn ton theon (glorifying God) is the proper response to the saving act; the crowd who saw it also praised God.
